parentQuestion: A patient can see objects perfectly but cannot recognize familiar faces, including their own family members. Which cortical area is most likely damaged?

scaffolds.q: Face recognition is a specialized brain function - not just general vision.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Humans are face experts - we have dedicated brain areas just for processing faces.

scaffolds.q: The fusiform gyrus is located on the ventral (bottom) surface of the temporal lobe.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The fusiform gyrus is part of the 'what' pathway for object recognition.

scaffolds.q: The fusiform face area (FFA) responds selectively and strongly to faces.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Show it a face - it lights up. Show it objects or houses - minimal response.

scaffolds.q: Prosopagnosia = face blindness - inability to recognize faces despite intact vision.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! Prosop- (face) + agnosia (without knowledge). They see faces but can't extract identity.

scaffolds.q: Prosopagnosia patients can see facial features but can't recognize WHO the face belongs to.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! They see eyes, nose, mouth - but can't extract identity. Even their own reflection is a stranger.

scaffolds.q: People with prosopagnosia often recognize others by voice, gait, or clothing.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! They develop workarounds because face recognition is broken.

scaffolds.q: If vision is intact but faces are unrecognizable, the fusiform face area is damaged.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! V1 is working (can see). Fusiform is damaged (can't recognize faces).

scaffolds.q: Sees perfectly + can't recognize faces = prosopagnosia = fusiform gyrus damage.
scaffolds.explain: Correct! The fusiform face area is the specialized face recognition module.

scaffolds.q: The answer is FUSIFORM GYRUS (fusiform face area) - the face recognition area.
scaffolds.explain: Yes! Not V1 (they can see), not prefrontal (executive function), not somatosensory (body sensation). Fusiform face area damage causes prosopagnosia - face blindness.
